topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the unfamiliar cadence hit me like a physical blow. I was making coffee, the morning routine humming along, when I overheard it. A phrase, fluid and melodic, spoken in my mother tongue. My breath hitched, the ceramic mug slipping slightly in my grip. I froze, the hot liquid forgotten. I turned slowly, my eyes fixed on him, a strange tightness constricting my chest. He was just laughing, a warm, easy sound, but beneath it, I sensed a shift, a hidden layer I hadn't noticed before. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. "What was that?" I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally high. He looked up, his smile faltering for just a second before he recovered, a flicker of something unreadable in his eyes.

[story 2]

It happened over dinner, the clinking of silverware suddenly seeming deafening. He was recounting a story from his travels, and then, without preamble, he interjected a sentence in my native dialect. It was perfect, the inflection spot-on, a language I hadn't heard spoken outside of recordings. My fork clattered onto the plate, a sharp, jarring noise in the sudden silence. I stared at him, my jaw tight. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't control. I felt a prickle of cold sweat tracing down my spine. I forced a casual tone, a strained lightness in my voice, but every muscle in my body was coiled, ready to spring away. "Are you... studying it?" I asked, the question coming out sharper than I intended.

[story 3]

I walked into the living room expecting quiet relaxation, but the air itself felt charged. He was reading a book, seemingly engrossed, yet the words spilling from his mouth were not English. They were intricate, lyrical, the very rhythm of my childhood home. A wave of disorientation washed over me, followed by a sudden, sharp need to retreat. I stopped mid-stride, my gaze locked on his mouth. My palms grew slick against the fabric of my trousers. I took a deliberate step back, putting more distance between us, my breathing growing shallow and quick. "Stop," I whispered, the word barely audible, but carrying the weight of sudden apprehension. He lowered the book, his expression shifting to one of mild surprise, and for a terrifying moment, I couldn't tell if he understood the warning in my tone.
